Investment Thesis

Why now

Discounted basis, local operating edge, market tailwinds, and a defined path to a 99-year lease create an unusually asymmetric entry point at The Tropics.

01

Exceptional Basis

The Ace Hotel & Swim Club sold for $60M in October 2022, or $335K per key. The Tropics — a mid-century Polynesian landmark — is available at $8M. That basis creates meaningful built-in downside protection.

02

Proven Operator, Local Edge

LBHG brings 50+ years of operating experience and 25 years in Palm Springs. Its 90-key BW Plus Las Brisas runs roughly 20 points above the comp set on occupancy — the local playbook behind this repositioning.

03

Desert Macro Tailwinds

Palm Springs draws 14.4M annual visitors and $7.4B of spending, while new supply remains constrained by zoning. That supports ADR growth and asset value for differentiated repositionings.

04

Seller Financing

The seller carries $3M at zero interest for three years, saving roughly $720K versus market. Combined with the existing $4M bank loan that is serviced through rent, the structure keeps early carry costs far below a conventional acquisition and gives the asset room to stabilize.

Deal Structure

Equity & capital structure

$4.0M of total equity consists of a $3.5M investment from Investor and a $500K co-investment from LBHG. LBHG serves as both co-investor and GP, with an 8% preferred return prioritizing Investor capital.

Investor

$3.5M capital contribution
75% of total equity capitalization
75% ownership with preferred economics

LBHG

$500K co-investment
25% economics comprised of 12.5% capital plus 12.5% promote
Co-investor and GP alignment from day one

Equity Allocation

How the $4.0M equity raise is allocated

The raise covers the upfront seller payment, renovation scope, working capital, and closing costs while preserving enough reserve for the Year 1–2 ramp. In the grounded case, the $350K reserve is sufficient because the modeled Year 1–2 operating shortfall totals about $256K.

Capitalization Path

From closing capital to full repositioning

This is the broader project capitalization view. Closing capital is intentionally simple: $8.0M purchase price, $4.0M senior debt in place, $3.0M seller carry at 0%, and $4.0M total equity. Initial CapEx is funded at close, and phased reinvestment is supported by retained operating cash flow before the refinance.

At closing

Purchase price: $8.0M. Existing senior debt: $4.0M. Seller financing: $3.0M at 0%. Upfront seller payment from equity: $1.0M. Initial renovation budget: $2.4M. Reserve: $350K. Soft costs and diligence: $250K.

Phased reinvestment before refi

Under the grounded case, Years 1–3 generate approximately $443K of cumulative retained operating cash flow after debt service. That cash can be recycled into phased room, public-area, pool, F&B, and systems upgrades without requiring a second equity raise. The reserve stays at $350K because the Year 1–2 operating shortfall totals only about $256K.

Investor Economics

Investor-first alignment

Investor contributes $3.5M for 75% ownership and LBHG contributes $500K for 25% economics. LBHG is both co-investor and GP, with hard dollars in the deal and a structure designed to protect Investor capital first.

01

8% Preferred Return

Investor receives first claim on distributable cash flow up to an 8% annual preferred return ($280K/year). In the grounded case, Years 1–2 are transition years, followed by Investor Yield of 15.0% in Year 3, 28.2% in Year 4, and 22.5% in Year 5.

100% → Investor
02

Operating Cash Flow Split

After the preferred return, remaining distributable cash flow splits 75/25.

75% Investor / 25% LBHG
03

Sponsor Buy-Up Option (Post-Refi)

After investor capital and pref are satisfied, LBHG has an ROFR to acquire an additional 26% at FMV and move to 51% ownership.

Target: LBHG 51% / Investor 49%
Strategy

Independent boutique resort

The Tropics is underwritten as an independent lifestyle resort, using its tiki heritage, design identity, and experiential F&B program to drive premium rates without franchise fees. Curated distribution, direct booking, and disciplined local programming support the grounded return case below.

What supports the return case

The underwriting assumes a measured two-year ramp, not an instant repositioning win. Years 1–2 absorb the transition, Year 3 becomes the inflection point, and the refinance occurs only after the 99-year lease path and operating proof are in place.

Even on that basis, the model still delivers a 33.9% IRR and 3.77× cash multiple without relying on aggressive exit assumptions.

Key takeaway: the three headline metrics are fully aligned: 22.5% Year 5 Investor Yield, 33.9% five-year IRR, and 3.77× cash multiple.
Capital discipline: initial CapEx is funded from the raise, while roughly $443K of retained operating cash flow through Year 3 supports phased reinvestment ahead of the refinance.

Addendum A

Path to the 99-year lease

A step-by-step path to securing long-term lease security on Agua Caliente tribal trust land — a core structural pillar of the transaction.

Recommended Path (Summary)

01
Obtain a Title Status Report (TSR) from BIA Palm Springs Agency to confirm land status (tribal trust vs. allotted trust), identify the landowner(s), and surface any encumbrances or title defects.
02
Determine governing framework: whether the Agua Caliente has approved HEARTH Act regulations for commercial leasing or if the standard BIA 25 CFR Part 162 (Subpart D — Business Leases) approval path applies.
03
Engage tribal realty office (Agua Caliente Trust Services) and BIA Palm Springs Agency early — request a Preliminary Review of proposed lease terms under §162.440 before formal submission.
04
Negotiate new 99-year lease terms with the individual allottee landowner or tribal authority. This is a new lease (not an extension) — the cleanest path. The existing ~27-year lease has already been extended once and may not be eligible for further extension.
05
Secure Indian landowner consent and tribal authorization (if tribal land). Prepare the full lease package per 25 CFR §162.438: executed lease, valuation/appraisal, NEPA documentation, entity documentation, insurance, performance bond, and environmental/archaeological assessments.
06
BIA reviews the complete package within 30 days of receipt for business leases (§162.440). If approved, the lease is recorded at the Land Title and Records Office (LTRO). If incomplete, BIA notifies parties of missing items.
07
Assignment strategy: recommend the seller (Harry) execute a concurrent assignment of the existing leasehold to our GP entity, while our GP entity simultaneously applies as the new leaseholder for the 99-year term. This avoids a gap in operational control.
08
NEPA compliance: initiate environmental assessment early (Month 1–2). BIA may adopt an existing EA/EIS under 43 CFR 46.320 if prior environmental work has been completed on the parcel. Hotel use is a continuation of existing use, which simplifies NEPA.
09
Valuation: BIA requires fair market rent determination. Engage an MAI appraiser with BIA trust land experience. Current ground lease terms (5% room revenue + 1.5% other revenue) should be validated against comparable leases in the Agua Caliente portfolio.
10
Lender coordination: SBA/conventional lenders require an executed, recorded lease with a remaining term exceeding the loan term by a meaningful margin. A 99-year lease satisfies all institutional lending requirements. Target: BIA approval and LTRO recording before refi application (2029).

When do we start the 99-year lease process?

Understanding the Deal Economics

This is a building lease with a purchase option, not a straight acquisition. Harry has $4M of existing bank debt and $4M of equity. LBHG pays Harry $1M upfront at lease commencement, leaving $3M due at purchase option exercise.

During the building lease, LBHG services Harry's $4M bank debt, while the remaining $3M carries at zero interest — saving approximately $720K over three years versus market. Harry remains the ground leaseholder and borrower of record until the purchase option is exercised.

Can we get the 99-year lease before we own the hotel?

Not cleanly. Under 25 CFR Part 162, the applicant for a new ground lease must have a legitimate basis to be the leaseholder. Because Harry controls the current ground lease, the cleanest route is a concurrent assignment and new 99-year lease application tied to the purchase option exercise, rather than a standalone filing during the building lease period.

RECOMMENDATION: Two-Phase Approach

Phase 1: Groundwork (Years 1–2)

During the building lease period, you can and should do the preparatory work:

— Get the TSR from BIA (understand exactly what you're dealing with)

— Identify the landowner and build the relationship

— Commission the environmental assessment (NEPA)

— Get the MAI appraisal for rent reasonableness

— Pre-negotiate lease terms with the landowner

— Request a BIA Preliminary Review of proposed terms

None of this requires control of the ground lease. It is preparatory work that materially de-risks the formal application later.

Phase 2: Formal Application (Year 3)

Begin the formal 99-year lease application ~12 months before your target refi date:

— Exercise the purchase option (or apply concurrently)

— Harry assigns existing ground lease to your entity

— Submit full lease package to BIA (all prep work done in Phase 1)

— BIA approves within 30 days (complete business lease package)

— LTRO records the new 99-year lease

— Apply for SBA/conventional refi with recorded lease in hand

With the groundwork complete, the formal application should be materially faster and cleaner than a cold-start 9–12 month process.

Why this works: Harry receives $1M upfront, LBHG services the $4M bank debt, and the remaining $3M carries at zero interest during the transition. The hotel is renovated, NOI is stabilized, and the lease process is advanced during Years 1–2. In Year 3, the purchase option, 99-year lease, and SBA refi can be executed in sequence, allowing the remaining $3M and bank debt to be taken out while returning investor capital.

Master Timeline: Building Lease → Purchase → 99-Year Lease → Refi

Simultaneously submit the pre-prepared 99-year lease package to BIA. Because all prep work is done, approval should take 2–4 months (not 9–12). This is why the Phase 1 groundwork matters.

BIA approves and records the 99-year lease at LTRO. Purchase closes — Harry receives his $8M. Apply for SBA refi with: recorded 99-year lease, 12+ months stabilized NOI, third-party appraisal, franchise agreement. Refi proceeds fund the purchase. Seller note retired. Investor capital returned. Everyone is made whole.
LEASE: ✓ Approved + Recorded
REFI TARGET ⟶
Key insight: Harry receives $1M upfront, LBHG services the $4M bank debt, and the remaining $3M carries at zero interest. That window is used to renovate, stabilize, and advance the 99-year lease file. By the time the purchase option is exercised, the BIA package is substantially prepared and the SBA refi can be paired with the acquisition takeout.

Extension vs. New Lease

Option A: Extend Existing Lease

Per 25 CFR §162, leases may be extended such that the total term does not exceed 99 years. However, the existing lease has already been extended once — and per the call transcript (Jan 2026), further extension is not possible through the standard path. The BIA contact confirmed that a new lease would be required to reach 99 years.

⚠ Not recommended — prior extension exhausted

Option B: New 99-Year Lease (Recommended)

Negotiate a new ground lease for up to 99 years directly with the Indian landowner, with BIA approval. This is the cleanest path: it produces a fresh lease that satisfies all institutional lending requirements, eliminates leasehold discount on exit valuation, and provides maximum term security for investors.

✓ Recommended — maximum financeability

Two paths to ownership

The building lease structure gives us a choice: take our time and save on interest, or move fast and own the asset outright. Both are viable. The right answer depends on your risk appetite, investor profile, and how quickly the BIA process can move.

Acquisition Plan A — Building Lease (3 Years)

Operate first. Buy later.

Take operational control in 2026 via the IMA / building lease. Pay Harry $1M upfront from his equity, service the $4M bank debt via rent, and let the remaining $3M carry at zero interest until the purchase option is exercised. Renovate, stabilize NOI, and execute the purchase in 2029 using SBA refi proceeds.

FINANCIAL BENEFIT

~$720K saved in interest — Harry's $3M equity balance at 0% interest over 3 years saves ~$240K/yr vs. market rate (8%). You're only paying interest on the $4M bank debt, which you service through rent.

Lower risk during renovation — you don't own the asset during the J-curve. If something goes wrong, your exposure is limited to operating losses + invested capex, not the full $8M purchase.

More time to prove NOI — 3 years of operating history gives lenders maximum confidence at refi. Stronger appraisal = more proceeds = better terms.

TIMELINE
2026: IMA → operational control → begin lease groundwork
2027: Renovation → pre-negotiate 99-yr lease terms
2028: Stabilize NOI → brand launch → 12-month track record
2029: Exercise option → 99-yr lease → SBA refi → close purchase
RISKS
Harry relationship — he carries for 3+ years; needs to stay aligned
Interest rate risk — rates could rise by 2029 refi
You don't own the asset — limited control over ground lease negotiations
BIA timing — if 99-yr lease delays, the refi and purchase slip
Acquisition Plan B — Accelerated (12 Months)

Buy fast. Own everything.

Run 90-day due diligence while simultaneously pursuing the 99-year lease with BIA. Target: close on the purchase + 99-year lease within 9–12 months. Get the SBA loan approved with the recorded 99-year lease. You own the hotel, the lease, and the debt from Day 1. No building lease middleman structure.

FINANCIAL BENEFIT

Immediate equity creation — $8M purchase price. Once the 99-year lease is recorded and renovation begins, the appraised value jumps significantly. You capture the spread between cost basis and market value from Day 1.

99-year lease = institutional asset — a recorded 99-year BIA lease eliminates the leasehold discount entirely. Comparable hotels trade at substantially higher valuations. Your cost basis has immediate mark-to-market upside.

SBA lock-in at today's rates — if rates drop further (forward curve ~3.2% by 2028), locking an SBA loan now could be advantageous vs. waiting 3 years when rates may be higher.

TIMELINE
M0: Execute LOI → begin DD + BIA engagement simultaneously
M1–3: Due diligence + TSR + NEPA + landowner negotiations
M3–6: Assemble full BIA lease package + SBA application
M6–9: BIA approval + LTRO recording
M9–12: SBA closes → purchase closes → you own everything
RISKS
BIA timing — process may not fit in 9–12 months
SBA underwriting — no stabilized NOI track record yet; lender may want projections
Higher debt service from Day 1 — carrying SBA loan during renovation J-curve
Harry must agree — different deal structure than what's been discussed

Head-to-Head Comparison

Plan B: Accelerated (12mo)

Equity upside hunters; rate-lock opportunists

Our recommendation: Proceed with Plan A (building lease). Harry receives $1M upfront, the $4M bank debt is serviced through rent, and the remaining $3M carries at zero interest — saving roughly $720K over three years. The structure lowers renovation-period risk, creates time to establish a proven NOI track record, and positions the SBA refi with 12+ months of operating evidence. Plan B remains available if Harry prefers a faster exit.

Risk Register — Top 10

1. BIA approval takes >12 monthsMed / High

Mitigation: Preliminary Review at M1; engage BIA liaison early; build 12-month buffer. Owner: GP Counsel

2. Landowner won't consent to new termsLow-Med / High

Mitigation: This is the single biggest structural risk. Mitigated during DD — landowner identity, willingness, and consent framework are confirmed before capital is committed. Engage early; offer competitive rent; tribal liaison advocacy. Owner: GP + Broker

3. NEPA triggers full EISLow / Med

Mitigation: Existing hotel = continuation of use; prior EA adoptable (43 CFR 46.320). Owner: Env. Consultant

4. Seller financing terms unresolvedMed / Med

Mitigation: Harry not in urgent need of capital; incentivize with upside participation. Owner: GP

5. Renovation costs exceed $4MMed / Med

Mitigation: 10% contingency; phased draw; competitive bidding. Owner: GP / PM

6. Y1–Y2 shortfall exceeds reservesMed / Med

Mitigation: Conservative occupancy (45–50%); $592K reserves; staged capex. Owner: GP / CFO

7. Distribution platforms not securedLow / Med

Mitigation: Multiple platforms available (Design Hotels, Tablet, Mr & Mrs Smith); not dependent on any single one. Owner: GP

8. Interest rates higher at refiMed / Med

Mitigation: Model uses 5.75% (conservative vs forward curve); scenario analysis provided. Owner: GP / CFO

9. Palm Springs market downturnLow / Med

Mitigation: Strict zoning limits supply; 14.4M visitors/yr; diversified demand. Owner: Market

10. Ground lease rent resetLow / Low

Mitigation: New 99-yr lease sets terms for full duration; negotiate fixed escalators. Owner: GP Counsel

Addendum B

Refinance scenario analysis

Three rate scenarios for the 2029–2030 refinance window, using current market data and forward curve pricing. All scenarios assume 65% LTV and 9.0% cap rate on stabilized NOI.

Market context (March 2026): Fed funds effective rate is 3.64%. Futures markets price a glide to ~3.2% by early 2028, firming to ~3.4% by 2030 (StreetStats / CME Fed Funds Futures, March 2026). FOMC Dec 2025 dot plot median for end-2027: 3.4%, with a range of 2.4–3.9% (FRED / St. Louis Fed). Conventional hotel lending spreads typically run 200–300bps over the risk-free rate for stabilized assets with strong lease security.

Refinance Scenario Analysis — Year 4 (2029)
MetricScenario A: LowerScenario B: BaseScenario C: Higher
Fed Funds Rate (2029)2.75–3.00%3.25–3.50%4.00–4.50%
Refi Rate (hotel debt)5.00%5.75%6.75%
Y4 NOI (Branded)$1,469K$1,469K$1,469K
Cap Rate8.50%9.00%9.50%
Property Value$17,282K$16,322K$15,463K
Refi Loan (65% LTV)$11,234K$10,609K$10,051K
Payoff Existing Debt$7,875K$7,875K$7,875K
Net Refi Proceeds (after 1% fee)$3,186K$2,571K$2,021K
Annual DS (Post-Refi)$720K$801K$894K
DSCR (Y5 NOI $1,777K)2.47x2.22x1.99x
Spread to Risk-Free+225bps+250bps+275bps
All scenarios maintain positive DSCR above 1.25x institutional threshold. Base case uses 5.75% refi rate — conservative relative to the forward curve (which implies fed funds ~3.2–3.4% in 2029, suggesting hotel rates of 5.2–5.7%). Higher scenario stress-tests a return to 2024-era rates. Sources: StreetStats.finance (March 2026 futures); FRED/St. Louis Fed (FOMC Dec 2025 SEP); CME FedWatch.

Refi Prerequisites

1. 99-year BIA lease executed and recorded at LTRO — non-negotiable for institutional lending.
2. Stabilized NOI (12+ months trailing) — lenders require proven operating history, not projections.
3. Third-party MAI appraisal supporting valuation.
4. Clean title and no material encumbrances.
5. Franchise agreement in place (Plan A) or strong independent track record (Plan B).

Refi Timing — Best / Base / Worst

Best case (Y3.5, mid-2029): BIA lease approved by end of 2027; renovation complete mid-2028; 12 months stabilized NOI by early 2029. Refi closes mid-2029.
Base case (Y4, early 2030): BIA takes 12 months; renovation extends into 2028; stabilization proven by mid-2029. Refi closes early 2030.
Worst case (Y5, 2031): BIA delays + renovation delays + slow occupancy ramp pushes stabilized NOI to late 2029. Refi closes 2031.

Addendum D

Why Plan A

Building Lease with Purchase Option — the case for operating first and buying later.

The economics favor patience

Harry has $4M of existing bank debt and $4M of equity. LBHG pays him $1M upfront at lease commencement, services the $4M bank debt through rent, and leaves the remaining $3M at zero interest for three years. That saves roughly $720K versus market and keeps more capital inside the repositioning.

Under Plan B (accelerated purchase), the deal takes on a $12M SBA loan from Day 1. At current SBA 504 rates (~5.8%), that implies roughly $906K–$928K of annual debt service against a grounded Year 1 NOI of only $162K. The early operating shortfall would be severe.

Under Plan A, our effective debt service during the building lease is only what we pay the bank on Harry's $4M loan — roughly $281K/year. That keeps the early carry light enough for the reserve to absorb the ramp while the renovation and lease strategy are executed. Even in the grounded case, the structure gives the hotel materially more breathing room than a Day 1 SBA takeout.

We don't own during the transition

Years 1 and 2 are when everything can go wrong: construction delays, permit issues, occupancy slower than projected, supply chain surprises. Under Plan A, if the renovation goes sideways or the market softens, our exposure is limited to operating losses and invested capex — not the full $8M purchase price. Harry still owns the asset. We still control operations. But we haven't written the check yet.

This is the core investor protection in the structure. The building lease allows the concept to be proven before ownership is assumed. In the grounded case, Year 1–2 reserve support is defined and limited, with the inflection arriving once NOI scales in Year 3.

The SBA conversation is completely different in Year 3

An SBA lender requires the ground lease term to equal or exceed the loan term. With 27 years remaining, a 25-year SBA loan technically fits — but no institutional lender wants a 2-year buffer on tribal trust land. The 99-year lease is what makes this bankable.

Under Plan A, by Year 3 the SBA lender is evaluating a recorded 99-year lease, 12+ months of stabilized NOI at $1.6M+, a completed renovation, and a clean operating track record. That is a fundamentally stronger underwriting case than showing up in Year 1 with projections and an unfinished BIA process.

The result is a cleaner credit story, stronger proceeds, and a more financeable refinance.

Preferred return is protected through the ramp

Under the grounded case, Year 1 and Year 2 are explicit transition years. Operating cash flow is negative $119K and $137K respectively, which is why the $350K reserve matters. The 8% investor preferred return is still the governing structure, but the model does not pretend the asset is fully ramped from Day 1.

By Year 3, NOI reaches $980K and Investor Yield reaches 15.0%. By Year 4, Investor Yield reaches 28.2%, and Year 5 still delivers a 22.5% operating yield while the five-year return lands at 33.9% IRR and 3.77× cash multiple.

When Plan B becomes the play

Plan B (accelerated purchase) becomes logical if Harry prefers a faster exit and does not want to wait three years. If that occurs, the LOI can preserve optionality by keeping the purchase path intact while referencing the operating structure as a fallback if the BIA timeline slips.

Absent that signal from Harry, Plan A remains the stronger path: lower risk, lower debt service through the J-curve, proven NOI at refinance, and roughly $720K of interest savings.

Sources & References

Data verification

Marriott Bonvoy membership: 271M members as of Q4 2025; member stays = 75% of U.S./Canada room nights. Source: Marriott International Q4 2025 Earnings Release (SEC filing, Feb 2026); Hotel Dive, Feb 2026.

Palm Springs visitor statistics: 14.4M visitors in 2023; $7.4B visitor spending; $9.0B total economic impact; 1-in-4 jobs tourism-supported. Source: Tourism Economics / Visit Greater Palm Springs 2023 Economic Impact Report.

Ace Hotel acquisition: 179-room property acquired by JRK Property Holdings from GFI Capital in October 2022 for $60M ($335K/key) via $350M Hospitality Fund. 5.04-acre campus, 7 buildings. Source: CoStar (Jan 2025); Hotel-Online, Oct 2022; Connect CRE, Oct 2022.

Ace Hotel brand sale: Ace Group International acquired by Seibu Prince Hotels Worldwide for ~$90M in September 2025. Source: Hotel Dive, Sept 2025; Travel Weekly, Sept 2025.

Caliente Tropics history: Built 1963, opened 1964 by Ken Kimes Sr. (motel #38 of 45; 5 were Polynesian-themed). Tiki carvings by Ed Crissman of Oceanic Arts (Whittier, CA) — 24 tikis carved from palm trunks on-site. Source: CalienteTropics.com/resort-history; AvoidingRegret.com; TheTikiChick.com; MyTiki.life.

BIA / Agua Caliente lease framework: Reservation encompasses ~28,000 acres. BIA Palm Springs Agency administers ~1,175 commercial leases, ~7,671 residential subleases, and ~11,118 timeshares (~20,000 total). Leases may not exceed 99 years. Source: BIA.gov/Palm Springs Agency; Epsten APC; 25 CFR Part 162.

BIA lease approval process: Business leases governed by 25 CFR Part 162, Subpart D. Required documents per §162.438. BIA 30-day review period for complete packages (§162.440). Preliminary Review available under §162.440. NEPA adoption provisions under 43 CFR 46.320. Source: eCFR.gov; BIA.gov.

Interest rate data: Fed funds effective rate 3.64% (March 2026). Futures curve: ~3.2% by early 2028, ~3.4% by 2030. FOMC Dec 2025 dot plot median for 2027: 3.4% (range 2.4–3.9%). Source: StreetStats.finance; FRED/St. Louis Fed; CME FedWatch.

SBA 504 requirements: Owner-occupancy 51% for existing buildings. Lease must be subordinate to SBA mortgage. Assignment of rents required as collateral. Source: SBA.gov/504-loans; BLP504.org; FFCFC.com.

LBHG credentials: 90-key BW Plus Las Brisas Hotel, downtown Palm Springs. ~85% occupancy (2023), $211 ADR (2024 TTM). Developer of the Year — Starwood Hotels & Resorts. Source: LBHG internal records.

Proforma and financial data: All financial projections derived from LBHG internal financial model (updated March 2026). Capital stack: $4M bank financing + $3M seller financing (0% interest) + $3.5M investor equity (75%) + $500K GP equity (25%). Building lease DS: $281K/yr. Post-refi DS: $871K/yr. Refi at 9% cap, exit at 8% cap.

STR market data: Palm Springs competitive set data from STR (Smith Travel Research), 2024 calendar year. Comp set occupancy: 53.9%; ADR: $253; RevPAR: $136. STR-informed base case uses Y1 assumptions of 48% occupancy / $158 ADR — below market, reflecting unrenovated product and transition. Source: STR, Inc. (2024).

Hotel cap rate data: U.S. luxury/upper-upscale hotel cap rates ~8.1% as of mid-2025, up from ~7.1% trough in 2021. Peak expected at ~8.1–8.2% in 2025–2026 with forecast compression to ~8.0% by 2028–2029. Resort/seasonal properties trade at wider cap rates than urban assets. Pre-pandemic luxury cap rates averaged mid-7% range (CoStar price index: ~7.4–7.7%, 2015–2019). Source: MMCG/CoStar database (Aug 2025); CBRE Hotels Americas Research; Hotel & Leisure Advisors (Jan 2026).

The Tropics — Palm Springs, CA  |  LBHG  |  Privileged & Confidential

Prepared March 2026  |  92 Keys · $8M Purchase · $3M Seller Financing at 0% · $4.0M Total Equity · 22.5% Y5 Investor Yield · 33.9% Investor IRR · 3.77× Cash Multiple
